summary: Apple rejected 37signals's Hey Calendar app from the App Store for the same reasons it rejected the Hey email app in 2020 — requiring an existing account to use. Gruber finds this baffling given the enormous bad publicity Apple suffered last time, and notes that Hey Calendar clearly qualifies as a free companion app under Apple's own guidelines, which explicitly cite email services as an example. He argues the rejection is especially foolish given the current global regulatory scrutiny of Apple's App Store practices. The timing is also terrible for Apple, which wants press attention focused on Vision Pro, not App Store controversies. Gruber sees zero upside for Apple — no revenue gain, only reputational and regulatory risk. Apple approved the app the next day.
key_points: Apple rejected Hey Calendar for the same 'doesn't do anything when you download it' reason it used against Hey email in 2020, despite the app clearly qualifying as a free companion app under Apple's own rule 3.1.3(f)
The regulatory environment has become far more hostile to Apple since 2020, making App Store controversies much more costly
Apple's timing is self-destructive — they want press coverage of Vision Pro, not another App Store bullying narrative
There is literally no financial upside for Apple in this rejection, only downside risk from bad publicity and regulatory ammunition
Apple reversed course and approved the app just one day later
money_quotes: It seems bonkers to me that after all the bad publicity that befell Apple in June 2020 over Apple's rejection of the Hey email app, they'd veto a Hey companion app — which requires the exact same type of account as Hey email — for the exact same reasons.
They should have just let it through, for the risk of bad publicity alone.
Apple doesn't want people writing articles like those (or this very one) this week. They want people writing articles about Vision Pro.
The stakes for Apple are much higher today than they were in 2020.

The last thing Apple wants is a news narrative along the lines of 'More Bullshit From Apple Trying to Squeeze Developers Into Giving Them a Cut of Revenue When the Developers Simply Want to Sell Subscriptions Directly to Customers Over the Web'.
But by rejecting Hey Calendar, they seem to be inviting such a narrative.
I just don't get it. Apple has nothing to gain by this — nothing, not a cent — but a lot to lose.
key_insight: Apple's rejection of Hey Calendar was strategically senseless — zero revenue upside, maximum reputational and regulatory downside — revealing an App Store review process that operates on bureaucratic autopilot rather than institutional self-interest.
